front: Novel: protagonist disguises as Desdichado ('the Disinherited Knight,' the Spanish word on his shield); defeats Templar Brian de Bois-Guilbert at tournament; defends the Jewish Rebecca from being burned as a witch; marries Rowena
Novel: Ulrica burns Torquilstone Castle, killing Reginald Front-de-Boeuf; Athelstane apparently rises from the grave; Bois-Guilbert dies of 'contending passions' in trial by combat
Novel: Rebecca heals the protagonist using a mysterious balsam taught by the sage Miriam; many Victorian readers wished she had married the protagonist instead of the Christian Rowena
Novel: the swineherd Gurth wears a brass collar with a Saxon inscription; Thomas Carlyle praises it in Past and Present

Author: Mark Twain blamed this novelist for the Civil War in Life on the Mississippi, saying he was 'in great measure responsible for the war' and created a 'Walter Scott disease' of sham chivalry in the South
Novel: Rena Walden's name in Chesnutt's The House Behind the Cedars is derived from this novel's Lady Rowena; a cross-burning scene in Thomas Dixon's The Clansman was also inspired by this novel
Novel: often regarded as the first historical novel; protagonist joins the Jacobite Rising of 1745 after visiting Donald Bean Lean and chieftain Fergus Mac-Ivor, falling for Flora
Novel: chieftain Fergus Mac-Ivor sings 'Lillibullero'; Edward sprains his ankle during a stag hunt after Flora plays a war ballad on the harp
Novel: Frank Osbaldistone travels to Scotland to track down Rashleigh; the title outlaw disguises himself as cattle dealer Campbell; Frank marries cousin Diana Vernon
Novel: comic relief from magistrate Baillie Nicol Jarvie and superstitious gardener Andrew Fairservice; set during the Jacobite Uprising of 1715
Novel: Jeanie Deans walks barefoot to London to seek a pardon for her sister Effie from Queen Caroline; opens with the Porteous Riots
back: The Heart of Midlothian (Sir Walter Scott)

front: Novel: Effie's illegitimate son 'the Whistler' later murders her husband
back: The Heart of Midlothian (Sir Walter Scott)

front: Poem: Roderick Dhu lights a burning cross, inspiring the KKK's practice; Ellen Douglas sings an Ave Maria; sparked the 19th-century Highland Revival
back: The Lady of the Lake (Sir Walter Scott)

front: Poem: Ellen Douglas's third song in a cycle based on this poem became known as the Schubert Ave Maria ('Ellens dritter Gesang')
back: The Lady of the Lake (Sir Walter Scott)

front: Poem: Rossini's opera La donna del lago is based on this; King James V disguises himself as 'Uberto' to seduce Elena (Douglas's daughter)
back: The Lady of the Lake (Sir Walter Scott)

front: Novel: title character stabs her husband Arturo on their wedding night and goes insane; Edgar of Ravenswood walks into quicksand in despair; inspired Donizetti's opera Lucia di Lammermoor
back: The Bride of Lammermoor (Sir Walter Scott)

front: Poem: contains 'What a tangled web we weave, when first we practice to deceive!'; published with copious antiquarian notes; ends with Ralph De Wilton marrying Clara de Clare
Author: organized George IV's 1822 tartan-heavy visit to Scotland, the first royal visit since Charles II; helped establish modern Scottish national identity
Author: cited fictional antiquarian 'Reverend Jonas Dryasdust' for historical background in novels; Carlyle also invoked Dryasdust
Author: Gyorgy Lukacs's Marxist reading praised this author's use of mediocre heroes as creating a new type of fiction used by Balzac and Tolstoy
Author: ruined when publisher Ballantyne & Co. collapsed; recounts this in a posthumously published journal
Author: published novels anonymously because fame as a poet was eclipsed by Byron; John Gibson Lockhart was this author's son-in-law
Author: Frederick Douglass took his surname from a character in one of this author's poems
Novel: Amy Robsart is tricked into falling to her death by Richard Varney; Anthony Foster dies in a gold room at Cumnor Place
